---
title: "Proof calculus and consistency"
qmd-prover:
  imports:
    - from: foundations.qmd
      use:
        - def-fol-signature
        - def-fol-terms
        - def-fol-formulas
        - def-fol-substitution
        - def-language-translation
        - lem-fresh-variable
---

::: {#def-hilbert-calculus .definition name="Classical first-order proof calculus" date="2026-07-12" export="def-hilbert-calculus"}
For a signature \(L\) (@def-fol-signature), with \(L\)-terms as in
@def-fol-terms, an \(L\)-derivation from a set \(\Sigma\) of \(L\)-sentences
(@def-fol-formulas) is a finite sequence of \(L\)-formulas.  Each line is:

1. a member of \(\Sigma\);
2. an instance (under uniform formula substitution) of a classical
   propositional tautology;
3. \(\forall x A\to A[t/x]\), where \(t\) is free for \(x\) in \(A\) as in
   @def-fol-substitution;
4. \(\forall x(A\to B)\to(A\to\forall xB)\), where \(x\notin\mathrm{FV}(A)\);
5. \(A[t/x]\to\exists xA\), where \(t\) is free for \(x\) in \(A\), and
   \(\forall x(A\to B)\to(\exists xA\to B)\), where
   \(x\notin\mathrm{FV}(B)\);
6. an equality axiom: \(t=t\), or
   \(s=t\to(C[s/x]\to C[t/x])\), where the displayed substitutions are
   free (this schema includes symmetry, transitivity, and substitutivity for
   functions and relations);
7. obtained by modus ponens from earlier \(A\) and \(A\to B\); or
8. obtained by generalization from an earlier \(A\), yielding \(\forall xA\).

Because assumptions are sentences by @def-fol-formulas, generalization has no
free-variable side condition on assumptions.  Write \(\Sigma\vdash_L A\) if
such a sequence ends in \(A\), and \(\vdash_L A\) when \(\Sigma=\varnothing\).
This is the proof calculus meant by the main goal.
:::

::: {#lem-proof-finitary .lemma name="Finitarity of derivability" date="2026-07-12" export="lem-proof-finitary"}
If \(\Sigma\vdash_L A\), some finite \(\Sigma_0\subseteq\Sigma\) satisfies
\(\Sigma_0\vdash_L A\).
:::

::: {.proof of="lem-proof-finitary"}
A derivation in @def-hilbert-calculus is finite.  Take \(\Sigma_0\) to be the
set of its lines justified as assumptions.  The same finite sequence is a
derivation from \(\Sigma_0\).
:::

::: {#lem-deduction .lemma name="Deduction theorem" date="2026-07-12"}
For a set \(\Sigma\) of sentences and sentences \(A,B\),
\(\Sigma\cup\{A\}\vdash_L B\) iff \(\Sigma\vdash_L A\to B\).
:::

::: {.proof of="lem-deduction"}
For the forward implication, induct on the finite derivation specified in
@def-hilbert-calculus.  If a line \(C\) is \(A\), use the propositional
tautology \(A\to A\).  If it is an axiom or belongs to \(\Sigma\), derive
\(A\to C\) from \(C\) and the tautology \(C\to(A\to C)\).  The modus-ponens
step follows from the tautology
\((A\to(C\to D))\to((A\to C)\to(A\to D))\).
If \(C\) was generalized to \(\forall xC\), the induction hypothesis gives
\(A\to C\); generalize to \(\forall x(A\to C)\), and apply quantifier axiom
4 of @def-hilbert-calculus to obtain \(A\to\forall xC\), since the sentence
\(A\) has no free variables by @def-fol-formulas.  The reverse implication is
one modus-ponens step using the added assumption \(A\).
:::

::: {#def-consistency .definition name="Syntactic consistency and completeness" date="2026-07-12" export="def-consistency"}
A set \(\Sigma\) of \(L\)-sentences, in the sense of @def-fol-formulas, is
*consistent* when there is no \(L\)-sentence \(A\) such that both
\(\Sigma\vdash_L A\) and \(\Sigma\vdash_L\neg A\) in the calculus
@def-hilbert-calculus.  It is *negation-complete* when for every
\(L\)-sentence \(A\), either \(A\in\Sigma\) or \(\neg A\in\Sigma\).  It is
*deductively closed* when, for every \(L\)-sentence \(A\),
\(\Sigma\vdash_L A\) implies \(A\in\Sigma\).
:::

::: {#lem-consistency-characterizations .lemma name="Consistency characterizations" date="2026-07-12" export="lem-consistency-characterizations"}
For a set \(\Sigma\) of sentences and any sentence \(A\), the following are equivalent:
(i) \(\Sigma\) is inconsistent in the sense of @def-consistency;
(ii) \(\Sigma\vdash_L\bot\), where \(\bot\) abbreviates the fixed
sentential contradiction
\((\forall z\,z=z)\wedge\neg(\forall z\,z=z)\);
(iii) \(\Sigma\vdash_L B\) for every sentence \(B\).
Moreover, if \(\Sigma\nvdash_L A\), then
\(\Sigma\cup\{\neg A\}\) is consistent.
:::

::: {.proof of="lem-consistency-characterizations"}
By @def-consistency, inconsistency of \(\Sigma\) means it derives some \(C\)
and \(\neg C\); a propositional tautology and two
modus-ponens steps in @def-hilbert-calculus then derive \(\bot\).  From \(\bot\),
the tautology \(\bot\to B\) derives every \(B\); (iii) plainly implies (i).
For the last assertion, inconsistency of
\(\Sigma\cup\{\neg A\}\) would give a derivation of \(\bot\).  By
@lem-deduction, \(\Sigma\vdash_L\neg A\to\bot\); classical propositional
logic proves \((\neg A\to\bot)\to A\), contradicting
\(\Sigma\nvdash_L A\).
:::

::: {#lem-chain-union-consistent .lemma name="Unions of consistency chains" date="2026-07-12"}
If \(\mathcal C\) is a nonempty chain under inclusion of consistent sets of
\(L\)-sentences, then \(\bigcup\mathcal C\) is consistent.
:::

::: {.proof of="lem-chain-union-consistent"}
If the union were inconsistent, @lem-consistency-characterizations and
@lem-proof-finitary would place a finite inconsistent subset in the union.
Each of its finitely many members belongs to some element of \(\mathcal C\);
since \(\mathcal C\) is linearly ordered, one element contains them all.  It
would be inconsistent by monotonicity of derivations in
@def-hilbert-calculus, a contradiction.
:::

::: {#lem-maximal-consistent .lemma name="Maximal consistent extension" date="2026-07-12" export="lem-maximal-consistent"}
Every consistent set \(\Sigma\) of \(L\)-sentences is contained in a
consistent, deductively closed, negation-complete set \(T\) of
\(L\)-sentences.
:::

::: {.proof of="lem-maximal-consistent"}
Consider the partially ordered set of consistent extensions of \(\Sigma\),
ordered by inclusion.  It is nonempty, and every chain has an upper bound by
@lem-chain-union-consistent.  Apply **Zorn's lemma** (the set-theoretic
principle: if every chain in a nonempty partially ordered set has an upper
bound, then a maximal element exists) to obtain a maximal consistent
extension \(T\).  Its hypotheses have just been verified.

If \(T\vdash_L A\) but \(A\notin T\), then \(T\cup\{A\}\) has exactly the
same consequences as \(T\) (replace each use of the new assumption by the
given finite derivation), so it is consistent, contradicting maximality.
Thus \(T\) is deductively closed.  For a sentence \(A\), if \(A\notin T\),
maximality makes \(T\cup\{A\}\) inconsistent.  By
@lem-consistency-characterizations and @lem-deduction,
\(T\vdash_L A\to\bot\), hence \(T\vdash_L\neg A\) by propositional logic;
deductive closure puts \(\neg A\) in \(T\).  Thus @def-consistency gives all
three claimed properties.
:::

::: {#lem-complete-theory-boolean .lemma name="Boolean laws in a complete theory" date="2026-07-12" export="lem-complete-theory-boolean"}
If \(T\) is consistent, deductively closed, and negation-complete, then for
sentences \(A,B\):
\(\neg A\in T\) iff \(A\notin T\), and
\(A\wedge B\in T\) iff both \(A\in T\) and \(B\in T\).
:::

::: {.proof of="lem-complete-theory-boolean"}
Negation-completeness from @def-consistency supplies at least one of
\(A,\neg A\); consistency forbids both because assumptions are derivable in
@def-hilbert-calculus.  The conjunction eliminations and introduction are
classical propositional tautologies, so deductive closure proves the second
equivalence.
:::

::: {#lem-constant-elimination .lemma name="Elimination of fresh constants" date="2026-07-12" export="lem-constant-elimination"}
Let \(L'=L\cup\{c_1,\ldots,c_n\}\), where the displayed constants are new.
If a set \(\Sigma\) of \(L\)-sentences derives an \(L\)-sentence \(A\) in
\(L'\), then \(\Sigma\vdash_L A\).
More generally, if distinct variables \(y_i\) are fresh for a finite
\(L'\)-derivation, replacing every \(c_i\) by \(y_i\) throughout yields a
valid derivation of the correspondingly translated conclusion from
\(\Sigma\).
:::

::: {.proof of="lem-constant-elimination"}
Using @lem-fresh-variable, choose distinct variables \(y_1,\ldots,y_n\) that
occur nowhere in the given finite derivation, and let \(T\) be the translation
of @def-language-translation replacing each \(c_i\) by \(y_i\).  Because \(T\)
alters only the constants \(c_i\), leaves every binder in place, and the \(y_i\)
occur nowhere in the derivation, it commutes with substitution on the material
of the derivation: for every formula \(C\), variable \(x\), and term \(t\) that
occur, \(T(C[t/x])=T(C)[T(t)/x]\), by induction on \(C\) using
@def-fol-substitution — each \(c_i\) is a constant untouched by \([t/x]\), and
the fresh \(y_i\) are captured by no binder of \(C\); moreover, since \(T\)
keeps the binders and introduces only the fresh \(y_i\), if \(t\) is free for
\(x\) in \(C\) then \(T(t)\) is free for \(x\) in \(T(C)\).  Apply \(T\) to every
line and verify each clause of @def-hilbert-calculus.  An assumption in
\(\Sigma\) is an \(L\)-sentence with no \(c_i\), so \(T\) fixes it; a
propositional-tautology instance maps to one because \(T\) commutes with
\(\neg\) and \(\wedge\); a quantifier or equality axiom instance maps to an
instance of the same schema by the commutation identity together with
preservation of the free-for side condition; modus ponens is preserved because
\(T(A\to B)=T(A)\to T(B)\); and a generalization step \(\forall z\) survives
because \(z\) occurs in the derivation, hence differs from every fresh \(y_i\),
so \(T(\forall z\,C)=\forall z\,T(C)\) is again a generalization.  The
translated sequence is therefore an \(L\)-derivation from \(\Sigma\) of
\(T\) applied to the conclusion, which is the general assertion.  When the
conclusion \(A\) is an \(L\)-sentence it contains no \(c_i\), so \(T\) fixes it
and the sequence derives \(A\) itself; this is the first assertion.
:::

::: {#lem-alpha-rename-provable .lemma name="Provable renaming of a bound variable" date="2026-07-12"}
Let \(A\) be a formula and let \(y\) be a variable that does not occur in
\(A\).  Then \(y\) is free for \(x\) in \(A\), the variable \(x\) is free for
\(y\) in \(A[y/x]\), \((A[y/x])[x/y]=A\), and
\[
 \vdash_L \exists x\,A \leftrightarrow \exists y\,A[y/x].
\]
:::

::: {.proof of="lem-alpha-rename-provable"}
Since \(y\) does not occur in \(A\), no binder of \(A\) is \(\exists y\), so by
@def-fol-substitution \(y\) is free for \(x\) in \(A\) and \(A[y/x]\) merely
relabels the free occurrences of \(x\) as \(y\).  Those are the only
occurrences of \(y\) in \(A[y/x]\), and each lies where \(x\) was free in
\(A\), hence under no binder \(\exists x\); therefore \(x\) is free for \(y\) in
\(A[y/x]\) and substituting \(x\) back gives \((A[y/x])[x/y]=A\).  For the
biconditional, quantifier axiom 5 of @def-hilbert-calculus in its first form
gives \((A[y/x])[x/y]\to\exists y\,A[y/x]\), that is
\(A\to\exists y\,A[y/x]\); generalizing over \(x\) and applying the
\(\exists\)-elimination form \(\forall x(A\to B)\to(\exists xA\to B)\) with
\(B=\exists y\,A[y/x]\) — where \(x\notin\mathrm{FV}(B)\) because
\(\mathrm{FV}(\exists y\,A[y/x])=\mathrm{FV}(A)\setminus\{x\}\) by
@def-fol-formulas — yields \(\exists x\,A\to\exists y\,A[y/x]\).  Symmetrically,
axiom 5 gives \(A[y/x]\to\exists x\,A\) since \(A=(A[y/x])[x/y]\); generalizing
over \(y\) and using \(\exists\)-elimination with \(y\notin\mathrm{FV}(\exists
x\,A)\) (as \(y\) does not occur in \(A\)) yields
\(\exists y\,A[y/x]\to\exists x\,A\).  A propositional tautology combines the
two implications into the stated equivalence.
:::

::: {#lem-henkin-witness-consistency .lemma name="Fresh witness preserves consistency" date="2026-07-12" export="lem-henkin-witness-consistency"}
Let \(\Sigma\) be a consistent set of \(L\)-sentences, let
\(\exists x A(x)\) be an \(L\)-sentence, and let \(c\) be a new constant.
Then \(\Sigma\cup\{\exists xA(x)\to A(c)\}\) is consistent in
\(L\cup\{c\}\).
:::

::: {.proof of="lem-henkin-witness-consistency"}
Write \(A(x)\) for the body, so that \(A(c)=A(x)[c/x]\).  Suppose the extension
is inconsistent.  By @lem-consistency-characterizations and @lem-deduction it
derives from \(\Sigma\), in \(L\cup\{c\}\), the negation of
\(\exists xA(x)\to A(c)\); propositional logic then yields derivations of both
\(\exists xA(x)\) and \(\neg A(x)[c/x]\).  Using @lem-fresh-variable, choose a
variable \(y\) occurring nowhere in this finite derivation, so in particular
\(y\) does not occur in \(A(x)\).  Replacing \(c\) by \(y\) throughout — the
one-constant case of @lem-constant-elimination — turns the derivation of
\(\neg A(x)[c/x]\) into a derivation from \(\Sigma\) of \(\neg A(x)[y/x]\).
Propositional logic gives \(A(x)[y/x]\to\bot\); generalization yields
\(\forall y(A(x)[y/x]\to\bot)\), and the \(\exists\)-elimination form of
quantifier axiom 5 in @def-hilbert-calculus (with \(y\notin\mathrm{FV}(\bot)\))
gives \(\exists y\,A(x)[y/x]\to\bot\), hence \(\neg\exists y\,A(x)[y/x]\).
Because \(y\) does not occur in \(A(x)\), @lem-alpha-rename-provable gives
\(\vdash_L\exists xA(x)\leftrightarrow\exists y\,A(x)[y/x]\), so
\(\Sigma\vdash_L\neg\exists xA(x)\).  This contradicts the derived
\(\exists xA(x)\), so \(\Sigma\) would be inconsistent, against the hypothesis.
Hence the extension is consistent.
:::
